artist_bio: Born 30th of August, 1932 in Agbarha-Otor, Delta state, Bruce Onobrakpeya is Nigeria’s foremost graphic illustrator, painter, printmaker and sculptor. He studied fine art at the Nigerian College of Arts, Science & Technology (NCAST), now Ahmadu Bello University, Zaria where he obtained a Diploma in Fine Arts, in 1961, specializing in painting and history of art. He was a pioneer member of the Zaria Art Society alongside other likeminded students including Uche Okeke, Yusuf Grillo, Demas Nwoko, Simon Okeke among others. As a renowned African art educator, he has travelled and taught in various institutions at home and abroad and has published numerous scholarly works. Onobrakpeya was also a pioneer member of the Society of Nigerian Artists (SNA). He is a recipient of Nigerian National Order of Merit Award for Humanities, Arts and Culture. In 2018, he was inducted into the Hall of Fame of the Society of Nigerian Artists (SNA) for his contribution to the growth of art in the country and his service to the nation.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Not for so Long
artist_bio: Kelechi Amadi-Obi (born December 29, 1969) is a renowned Nigerian photographer, painter, and publisher of Mania Magazine. His work has gained international recognition, with exhibitions such as Snap Judgment: New Positions in Contemporary African Photography in New York (2006) showcasing his talent. A trailblazer in celebrity photography, Amadi-Obi has played a pivotal role in elevating Nigerian photography on the global scene, with Vogue hailing him as "a major force in the creative scene in Nigeria." In Not for So Long, Amadi-Obi presents a striking image of a young African woman, lying on a bed in a contemplative, seated position. The artwork, rendered in black and white, uses subtle shading to emphasize the elegance of her ebony-black hair and the curve of her back, turned away from the viewer. The subdued tones and intimate pose suggest an air of quiet anticipation, as if she is waiting for someone. The simplicity of the composition, paired with the emotional depth conveyed by her posture, invites viewers to engage with her inner thoughts and emotions, creating a moment of quiet reflection in the viewer’s experience.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art

title: Digital Armour
description: Digital Armour captures the importance of technology in the 21st century Digital Age. With numerous technological advancements and discoveries in different fields over the years and the global consciousness to embrace these new findings by all — nations, corporations and individuals alike — technology has seemingly become indispensable in our daily lives and interactions. As a matter of fact, we are all surrounded by it. The artist uses different parts of electrical appliances and the bull, a beast known for its agility, enormous energy and power as symbols to talk about the powerful influence of technology in the modern day society. The piece emphasizes the need for all nations, corporations and individuals to develop and keep abreast with advancements and "arm" themselves with new innovations in technology to remain relevant, competitive and powerful like the Bull in the digital age.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Afikpo Mask
description: Afikpo Mask, Ndidi Dike attempts to explore the dynamic aesthetics of the local masquerade, Mmonwu, of the Afikpo people of Ebonyi state, south-east Nigeria. The different outlooks of the mask are shown on the eleven wooden panels, each with unique facial attributes. Ndidi stylishly identifies with the Uli insignia which the Nsukka Art School was famous for. The presence of uli lines and patterns on the body of the panels gives each mask a unique aesthetic appeal. Her colours, though quiet, draw attention for closer viewing and admiration.
artist_bio: Born 1960 in London, Ndidi Dike showed very early interest in art as a child taking art classes in primary school. After her secondary school she relocated to Nigeria for her tertiary education at the University of Nigeria Nsukka (UNN) where she obtained a Diploma in Music Education (voice) and B.A in Fine and Applied Arts (majoring in Mixed Media Painting) in 1984. Passing through the walls of the famous Nsukka Art School had a great influence in her art. She kick-started her career as a solo artist in her hometown, Owerri, right after her one-year mandatory National Youth Service in 1985. Her first solo exhibition Mixed Media Expose was hosted in 1986. Ndidi is one of Nigeria’s leading female artists and has worked in diverse media all through her professional career spanning over three decades. She is a member of the Society of Nigerian Artists (SNA) and also belongs to the Guild of Fine Arts, Nigeria (GFAN). In
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Mother and Child
description: Mother and Child is an illustration of a breastfeeding mother and her child. The mother sits on a chair with the busy, suckling baby in her arms. She is dressed (like a typical Nigerian woman) in a wrapper, a blouse and a nicely tied scarf covering her head. The drawing radiates the feelings of passion, love and tenderness and the warm connection one normally finds between the duo in reality.
artist_bio: Eke Okaybulu was a self-thought Nigerian artist from the then Eastern Nigeria. He had little formal training unlike a lot of his contemporaries at the time who had college degrees in art and graphic designs from reputable tertiary institutions like the Nigerian College of Art, Science and Technology (NCAST), now Ahmadu Bello University, Zaria and Yaba College of Technology, Lagos which had European teachers at time. However, he was influenced by DLK Nnachi, Akinola Lasekan, Aina Onabolu and other students of Kenneth Murray, one of the first British trainers in Eastern Nigeria in the early 20th century. He demonstrated keen interest in graphic art and illustration and would go on to win a government scholarship to London in 1952. Part of his notable awards is a First Class Certificate of Merit, Drawings and paintings, Art and Crafts Exhibition, in Port Harcourt (1944). He was inducted Posthumously as a Fellow of the Asele Institute, Nimo in 1985.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Untitled
artist_bio: Ben Osawe was the son of a traditional carver. His father was one of the sculptors in the palace of Oba Eweka II of Benin. From his father, Ben Osawe received his first training in the art of carving. In 1956, he left Nigeria for London where he had his Formal training in the School of Graphic Arts (1956-59) and in Camberwell School of Art (1960-65). While in London, he was selected to represent Nigeria at the Commonwealth Festival in Glasgow in 1965 and in 1966 he returned to Nigeria. In his sculptures, Ben liked to depict the inconsistency in human nature. He tries to capture the serenity and sometimes agony that can disrupt one's countenance. He produced a lot of artworks in wood which is the major medium he worked in. This piece depicts a stylized profile of a lady. Her elongated neck reflects poise and elegance while her facial features portrayed in geometrical shapes and forms exudes lucid frankness.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Tranquility
description: Tranquility, Abiodun depicts dawn at a local river scene that is distant from a city. The river is surrounded on its banks by lush vegetation and trees and its mood is filled with so much calmness and serenity, a typical situation in local rivers before the resumption of usual activities during the day. Abiodun shows proficiency and excellence in his mature usage of colours for realistic expression and giving life to the painting. The setting appears very ideal for clear focus and meditation in the early hours of the morning. One could literally hear the sounds of the gentle ripples on the surface of the water and feel the freshness of the air from the surrounding trees and grasses.
artist_bio: Abiodun Olawale Olaku is a contemporary Nigerian painter born in Lagos in 1958. He obtained an Ordinary National Diploma (OND) and a Higher National Diploma (HND) from the prestigious Yaba College of Technology in 1979 and 1981 respectively, majoring in painting. Over the years, Abiodun has established himself as one Nigeria’s most accomplished oil painters due to his very detailed, naturalistic expression of landscapes and Nigerian cities. His style shows meticulous attention to detail that draws every viewer closer to their imagination in reality than ever. His unique bird’s-eye rendition of landscapes shows how he has mastered his craft through utter diligence and draughtsmanship over time. Abiodun has participated in numerous exhibitions in Nigeria including the "Images of the Nigerian Nation," National Theatre, Lagos, 1990 among many others. He is a member of the Society of Nigerian Artist (SNA). In
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Abeokuta from Olumo View
description: Landscape painting depicting hilly terrain of Abeokuta, the capital city of Ogun state in Nigeria. This view of the town is gotten from Olumo rock, one of Nigeria's geographical landmarks and tourist location.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Ishere Village
description: Ishere Village, Oshinowo presents a rustic scene of village life, with rusted corrugated iron roofs blending with the earthy tones of the soil. The people move about, going about their daily lives, while boats—some damaged—lay in the foreground, hinting at the fishing activities of the community. The painting captures the tranquility and beauty of rural existence.
artist_bio: Kolade Oshinowo is a prolific contemporary Nigerian painter and educator, having taught at Yaba College of Technology for over 35 years. His art explores themes of urban and rural life, and he has addressed social and political issues through his paintings. Oshinowo's work captures the richness of Nigerian culture and everyday life, often portraying the peaceful simplicity of village life. In
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art

title: Choice of Freedom
artist_bio: Olawunmi Banjo is a self-taught Lagos-based contemporary painter born in 1985. With no formal training in art, she was inspired by the works of Aina Onabolu. Known for her surrealism and hyper-realism, Banjo’s paintings often explore the emotional states and hidden realities that confront individuals and society. She has exhibited both locally and internationally. In this painting, a figure wrapped in checkered black-and-white fabric struggles to free herself. The fabric represents bondage, while the thorns beneath her feet symbolize the harsh reality she’s trying to escape. Her sideways gaze and grappling with the fabric highlight her decision to fight for freedom.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art

title: The Emir in Council
description: The Emir in Council depicts the traditional setup when an emir attends to his council. The figures all sit around the emir while he stands and speaks to them with a stick in hand.
artist_bio: Along with Aina Onabolu and Ben Enwonwu, Akinola Lasekan was one of the pivotal instruments in the evolution of Modern Nigerian art. Born into the Oladetimi family in Owo, Ondo State in 1916, he had little access to formal education and this difficulty encouraged him to form a training school of his own where he taught other young aspiring artists like himself. As a citizen of his times, he was affected by the British colonial rule ongoing at the time and lent his voice through the "West African Pilot" a newspaper publication where he published satirical illustrations depicting the ills of the colonial administration. His paintings carry on this cartoon element which he explored while working for the West African Pilot.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: The Flutist
description: The Flutist, Egonu graphically depicts a young man smoking a pipe with a flute resting on his lap. His use of cool hues like blue, yellow and purple gives the viewer a feeling of serenity.
artist_bio: Born in Nigeria in 1931, Uzo Egonu went on to settle in Britain in the 1940s. He studied Fine Arts and Typography at the Camberwell School of Arts and Crafts, London from 1941 to 1952. Here he became very attune with the ideas in his immediate environment while keeping abreast of African political struggles back home. He is acclaimed to have helped redefine the boundaries of modernism, thereby challenging the European myth of the naive, primitive African artist. In
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Effusive Series
artist_bio: Nyemike Onwuka, born in 1972, is a renowned Nigerian postwar and contemporary painter known for his vibrant and expressive works. studied general arts and majored in painting at Auchi Polytechnic, Edo State. He also studied animation and visual effects at Witwatersrand University, Johannesburg, South Africa. Onwuka’s art is deeply rooted in his Nigerian heritage, often reflecting his homeland's rich cultural narratives and traditions. His ability to capture the essence of human experiences and emotions has made his work resonate with a wide audience. This artwork portrays a woman with her hair elegantly wrapped in a scarf, and her lips painted red, complemented by matching earrings and a necklace. Her eyes are closed, her expression conveying deep emotion as if lost in distant thought. The piece must be a part of the artist's “Effusive” series, which explores themes of cultural identity, resilience, strength, connection, and community. This series is particularly distinguished by its vibrant use of color and emotion, capturing the essence of human experiences and cultural narratives.

title: Waiting II
description: Waiting II is the second in a series of painting by Diseye portraying a group of women patiently waiting for a haulage task, as generally seen in market places. Unlike the first in the series, the three women look tired and exhausted from the busy day of strenuous haulage activities. They all seem to be sleeping with the first sitting inside her basin with her face covered with her scarf, the second lying and sleeping on the back of her basin, while the last is sitting on the floor with her arms anchored on her knees and covering her face. The texture of the work characterized by blend of smooth and rough, “sketchy” brush strokes on the bodies of the characters and dripping paints of different hues all over its surface. The painting reflects on the importance of taking a nap and resting during and after stressful work as it celebrates the resilience and doggedness of women who strive daily to earn a living and sustain their families.
artist_bio: Diseye Tantua is a contemporary Nigerian painter born in 1974 in Accra, Ghana to a Nigerian father and a Ghanaian mother. He obtained a Bachelor’s Degree in Art from the Department of Fine and Applied Art at the Rivers State University of Education, now Ignatius Ajulu University, Rivers State. Diseye’s art is famous for his fusion of bold, bright colours and, most times, graphic symbols and expressive texts in a rendition style known as “Afro Pop Art”. His themes are generally centered around urban and social life, inspired by the daily struggle to survive and make ends meet in the city. Diseye lives and works in Port-Harcourt, Rivers State and his works have been widely exhibited at home and abroad.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: The Muslim Beauty
description: The Muslim Beauty, is a portrait of a young beautiful lady, probably of Yoruba decent, elegantly dressed in a yellow Muslim Hijab. Her outlook shows simplicity, decency and humility. Islam is one of the dominant religions in Western Nigeria.
artist_bio: Famous for naturalistic portraiture, Akinola Lasekan was one of the early pioneers of modernism in Nigerian art in the 20th century. He and his peers were the first set of students to receive European art education. They would go on to become the first indigenous art educators and would spread the gospel of art in government colleges and mission schools across the country in the 1940’s. Besides teaching in schools, he made personal strides in democratizing art and making art education/training more accessible to people by setting up the Lash Studio Correspondence School in Lagos in 1940 where he organized and offered diploma art training courses via Correspondence. Some of the courses offered were book illustration, commercial art, colouring, landscape and figure drawing. Pictorial Naturalism was incorporated and strongly emphasized in his correspondence courses. This had a lot of influence on numerous artists in the country including the likes of Abayomi Barber whose surrealism was founded on Lasekan’s naturalism. In 2018, he was posthumously inducted into the Society of Nigerian Artists (SNA) Hall of Fame for his distinguished service to the society and the nation and his immense contribution to Lash, as he preferred to address himself for short, loved to explore themes surrounding Yoruba myths and culture, the environment and life around him in his paintings.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Waiting I
description: Waiting I shows a group of women sitting and patiently waiting for a haulage task, normally found in rural and urban market places. Metallic basins which serve as troughs for loading customer goods on their heads can be seen in the background. The second woman on the left can be noticed sitting on her own basin. As they patiently wait to be patronized, the two women on the right can be noticed to be chatting while the others seem indifferent in their outlook. Diseye displays an excellent maturity in his use of colours for this painting as he explores the theme of Nigerian and African women whose spirit of hardwork, resilience, commitment and perseverance is second to none as they strive daily to make ends meet for their families.
artist_bio: Diseye Tantua is a contemporary Nigerian painter born in 1974 in Accra, Ghana to a Nigerian father and a Ghanaian mother. He obtained a Bachelor’s Degree in Art from the department of Fine and Applied Art at the Rivers State University of Education, now Ignatius Ajulu University, Rivers State. Diseye’s art is famous for his fusion of bold, bright colours and, most times, graphic symbols and expressive texts in a rendition style known as “Afro Pop Art”. His themes are generally centered around urban and social life, inspired by the daily struggle to survive and make ends meet in the city. He lives and works in Port-Harcourt, Rivers State and his works have been widely exhibited at home and abroad.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Takeoff Point
artist_bio: Alex Shyngle captures the typical mood of locations of this nature which are common in the riverine areas and coastlines of Nigeria. Despite not being as popular as other means and types of transportation, water transportation is still very much in use in the country today.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Emanating
description: Emanating, the artist uses shades of grey, white, and black to depict a central object, evoking a sense of abstraction and depth. The word "emanating" means "to originate or to spread out," which is reflected in the artwork's composition. The shades and textures in the piece appear to flow outward, symbolizing the extension of energy, ideas, or emotions from a core source. This work invites viewers to contemplate the essence of creation and how influences radiate from a single point, shaping the surrounding environment.
artist_bio: Odun Orimolade, born in 1976, is a Nigerian visual artist whose work spans painting, drawing, collage, etching, sculptural installation, and performance. She challenges viewers to reconsider their roles in experiencing art. Orimolade graduated from Yaba College of Technology and holds a Postgraduate Diploma and a Master’s in Visual Art from the University of South Africa, where she is also pursuing a PhD. Her experimental approach frequently highlights drawing and investigates themes of overlapping realities and the influence of external forces on human behavior. Based in Lagos, Orimolade has exhibited widely in solo shows and serves as a lecturer at her alma mater. She is also deeply involved in community work, facilitating art workshops and mentoring young artists. In
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art

title: Untitled
description: Untitled piece shows a classic creative experiment typical of El Anatsui, done with wooden panels. He normally cuts his wood into panels with a chainsaw before he engraves signs and motifs all over them, then paints them with colours. The panels on the work have been rounded and smoothened. Some of them are not fully rounded in shape like others. The black engraved motifs are symbols of the uli art.
artist_bio: Emmanuel Kwami Anatsui is a painter and sculptor born in Anyako, Ghana in 1944. He obtained a B.A, majoring in Sculpture, in 1968; a postgraduate diploma in art education in 1969; and won the best student of the year award, 1969 all from the College of Art, University of Science & Technology, Kumasi, Ghana. He started out as a lecturer at the Specialist Training Centre, Winneba, Ghana between 1969-1975. He then relocated to Nigeria and became a sculpture professor at the Department of Fine and Applied Art of the prestigious University of Nigeria, Nsukka in 1975. Anatsui has spent the better part of his career in Nigeria and has explored different media including wood, clay and found objects in his creative, sculptural experiments as an artist. His first solo exhibition in Nigeria was the "Wooden Wall Plaques by El Anatsui," Asele Art Gallery, Nsukka, in February, 1976. His works are filled with Ghanian motifs together with symbols of uli, nsibidi and adinkra as he explores themes surrounding Nigerian and Ghanaian cultures and heritage. Recently, he has shifted his focus to installation art and has garnered so much international acclaim and recognition for his niche “bottle-top installations”. He is currently the biggest and most celebrated artist from Africa. This
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: First Station
description: First Station is the first in Onabrakpeya’s series depicting the Stations of the Cross, which chronicles the suffering and crucifixion of Jesus Christ. Onabrakpeya, however, brings a unique cultural reinterpretation to this biblical narrative. In his version, Jesus is dressed in traditional African attire, while the Roman soldiers who jeer and guard him are depicted in British colonial uniforms. This deliberate artistic choice underscores the theme of oppression, drawing a parallel between Christ’s suffering and the colonial experience in Africa. In the artwork, Jesus is portrayed carrying his cross, while Pontius Pilate, on the far right, is seen washing his hands, symbolizing his attempt to absolve himself of responsibility for Christ's crucifixion. Through his use of African cultural elements and the colonial references, Onabrakpeya adds layers of meaning to the religious story, emphasizing the relevance of faith, suffering, and liberation in both African history and Christian theology.
artist_bio: Bruce Onabrakpeya, born in 1932, is a renowned Nigerian artist recognized for his pioneering contributions to printmaking and mixed media. With a career spanning decades, Onabrakpeya has skillfully blended traditional African themes with modern techniques, exploring concepts of identity, spirituality, and cultural heritage. His work has greatly influenced the Nigerian art landscape, particularly through his mentorship and role in fostering a resurgence of contemporary African art.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art

title: The Goat Market
description: The Goat Market, as the title implies, shows a local Nigerian market where goats, cows and sheep are the only commodities traded. Markets of this nature are at their peak during religious celebrations (eg Christmas, Easter, Salah etc) and cultural festivities in the country as the demand for meat is usually very high during those periods of the year. Though it is more synonymous with the Northerners, Livestock farming is a very common practice in the country as it is very a lucrative business.
artist_bio: Born 1971, Abdulsalam Abdulkareem is a native of Bida, Niger state. He studied Fine Art at the Yaba College of Technology where he obtained an OND (1998) and a HND (2000), majoring in painting.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Nomads
description: Nomads portrays a herd of men and women walking through what looks like a desert with a few surrounding trees. They can be noticed carrying their luggage on their heads as they all head east on the painting. Oshinowo is probably trying to express the nomadic lifestyle common among the Fulanis of northern Nigeria who are known for their constant migration and nomadic nature in general. The choice of colours is dull, yet warm while texture feels rough with gentle brush strokes. Migration is the central theme expressed in the painting.
artist_bio: Kolade Adekunle Oshinowo is a well-respected contemporary Nigerian painter and teacher born in Ibadan in 1948. He obtained a Bachelor of Arts in Fine Arts from Ahmadu Bello University, Zaria in 1972, majoring in painting. He was a teacher at King’s College, Lagos (1972-74). In 1973, he started lecturing part-time at the Yaba College of Technology (YABATECH), Lagos, at the persuasion of Yusuf Grillo who assigned him to take a weekly class on pictorial composition. Eventually he joined the institution full time in 1974 and would spend the next 35 years there in service, teaching and inspiring generations of talent to become great artists. From 1990, he was the chief lecturer and director, School of Art, Design & Printing, YABATECH. Oshinowo is very passionate about his profession as an artist and believes in the unique role of art for a better society. He would instill these ideals and values upon his students making them see their profession beyond illustrations, drawing, painting and sculpting. As a prolific painter he loves exploring themes on urban life, events/activities in his surroundings and everyday life in the larger society. He also seeks to address different social and political issues in the society in his works. In 2018, Oshinowo was inducted into the Hall of Fame of the Society of Nigerian Artists (SNA) for immense contribution to the practice and teaching of visual art in Nigeria and his service to the nation.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Market Place
description: Market Place is a classic landscape by Kolade Oshinowo showing an open local market setting in a village saturated with colours. One sees the very busy nature of such markets with people moving randomly around, either hawking or looking for things to buy on a typical market day. The traders can be seen sitting on chairs, surrounded by large baskets containing their stock on the floor. Some of them have umbrellas to cover themselves from the scotching heat of the sun while others don’t. Some of the women can be noticed with their grown children who assist in selling their goods while a few have their babies either tied to their back with a wrapper or in their arms. The market seems to be at the heart of the small village as resident houses in brown can be seen surrounding it and spreading into the horizon of the painting. The horizon is also filled with lush trees in different shades of green as well. Oshinowo shows so much technicality and skill in the surrealistic portrayal of this local Nigerian landscape.
artist_bio: As a prolific painter, Kolade Adekunle Oshinowo loves exploring themes on rural and urban life, events/activities in his surroundings and everyday life in the larger society. The
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Prince Yemisi Adedoyin Shyllon
description: Prince Yemisi Adedoyin Shyllon hails from Abeokuta, in Ogun State of Nigeria, from the Sogbulu and Ogunfayo lineage of the Laarun royal ruling house of Ake in Egbaland. He is a graduate of Engineering (University of Ibadan), of Law (University of Lagos) and an MBA degree (Obafemi Awolowo University). He is a Nigerian Chartered Stockbroker and Fellow of several chartered professional bodies. Prince Shyllon served as a manager of Tractor and Equipment (division of UACN PLC conglomerate Nigeria). In 1983, he was appointed executive director and for some years was also the legal adviser of Nigerite Ltd in Nigeria, a subsidiary of the ETEX Group of Belgium. He commenced investing in the stock market in his university undergraduate days and continued investing in real estate, shares, business startups and, of course, artworks. He commenced collecting artworks from his undergraduate days at the university. Prince Shyllon established his art foundation - Omooba Yemisi Adedoyin Shyllon Art Foundation (OYASAF), in 2007. The OYASAF art collection is Nigeria’s largest private art collection. It holds over 7,000 artworks of all genres as well as over 55,000 photographic images of Nigeria’s fast disappearing cultural festivals. OYASAF sponsors scholars, curators and artists from within and outside Nigeria, to study his collection and research into Nigerian art and culture. In 2012, he endowed the Yemisi Shyllon Professorial Chair for Fine Art and Design at the University of Port-Harcourt, Nigeria and the on-line journal of African art, “The OYASAF Journal of Art (TOJA)”. OYASAF has donated life size sculptural monuments to the Freedom Park in Lagos, to the Universities of Lagos and Ibadan and to other public places and institutions in Nigeria. Prince Shyllon is the joint author of a book on the Yoruba sculptor, Lamidi Fakeye. He granted 1,000 artworks to the Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art at Pan Atlantic University. His works form the core of the museum’s collection. Programmes
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Untitled
artist_bio: Hakeem Salaam is an acclaimed Nigerian photographer recognized for his expertise in documentary, travel, landscape, portrait, and fashion photography. He began his journey in photography in 1989 and became widely recognized in 2001 when he transitioned to capturing real people. Over the years, he has been involved in prominent photography projects, including Beautiful Nigeria and Vitiligo Portraits, and has received the Fashion Photographer of the Year Award. He has exhibited work at the 2019 Ake Festival and is a Canon Influencer and trainer. In this powerful photograph, a mother and child are depicted in an Internally Displaced Persons (IDP) camp. The image conveys raw emotion, with the mother gazing directly at the viewer, asking an unspoken question. The composition and colors amplify the emotional weight of the scene, highlighting themes of displacement and survival.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art

title: The Architecture of the Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art
description: The Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art is the first purpose built, privately owned art museum in Nigeria. The Museum is located in Lagos and part of the Pan-Atlantic University. The Museum opened to the public in 2020 and was designed by Spanish-Nigerian architect and Director Jess Castellote. The architecture is inspired by the principles of the buildings at the Pan-Atlantic University; fitness for purpose, flexibility, sustainability and character. The stained concrete outer walls was inspired by the red mud houses of earlier civilizations, including the ancient Benin Kingdom. The main building houses the art galleries and the gazebo is used for educational activities. The 900 sqm Museum hosts over 1000 artworks from Prince Yemisi Shyllon’s art collection. The Museum holds one of the most comprehensive and diverse art collections in Nigeria. Visitors are invited to learn more about art from the ancient kingdoms Ife, Nok and Benin. Visitors can also explore art by contemporary artists including Ben Enwonwu, Bruce Onobrakpeya and Nike Davies-Okundaye.

title: Freedom and Truth
artist_bio: Morohunmubo Olayemi Afolabi is a mixed-media artist whose work is characterized by intricate graphic lines and flowing forms, drawing on her background in architecture. Through abstraction and collage, she layers stories and personal narratives to build immersive, richly textured worlds. With over 25 years of experience in art, interior design, and architecture, Mubo is trained as both an architect and artist. She studied at the Inchbald School of Interior Design and the University of Creative Arts in the UK, as well as Castello Valentino, Turin, Italy, as part of the ERASMUS project. Mubo also holds an MBA in Digital Marketing and is a member of several professional bodies, including the Royal Institute of British Architects, the Architects Registration Council of Nigeria, and the Nigerian Institute of Architects. This vibrant painting bursts with colors—blue, purple, orange, and more—creating a fantastical, utopian world. Mubo invites the viewer to explore her dreamlike landscapes, blending architecture with imagination.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art

title: Fifth Station
description: Fifth Station, the biblical moment when Simon of Cyrene helps Jesus carry the cross is reinterpreted with a cultural twist. True to his style, Onabrakpeya merges traditional African elements with the religious narrative, emphasizing the local and global significance of the story. Simon of Cyrene, a key figure in this station, is portrayed in African attire, underscoring his cultural roots and aligning him with the African audience. His assistance to Jesus symbolizes the universal theme of compassion, transcending cultural and geographical boundaries. In contrast, a guard in the scene is dressed in British colonial uniform, a recurring symbol in Onabrakpeya’s Stations of the Cross series. This juxtaposition between African and colonial imagery adds layers of meaning, suggesting themes of oppression, empathy, and shared suffering. Onabrakpeya’s artistic choices not only highlight the historical moment but also invite reflection on the struggles faced by African societies under colonial rule, drawing parallels between the biblical narrative and the African experience. Through this artwork, he transforms a religious story into a broader commentary on humanity, sacrifice, and cultural identity. This Fifth Station blends the sacred with the socio-political, showcasing Onabrakpeya’s unique ability to contextualize spiritual themes within African history and culture, making the piece relevant to contemporary audiences.
artist_bio: In Bruce Onabrakpeya’s
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art

title: Portuguese Soldier
description: Through the Benin punitive expedition of 1897 the first bronze works from Africa were recorded. These bronze works are known collectively as Benin Art. They were first discovered when the spoils of the Benin punitive expedition caught the attention of the European public after the expedition. The first contact of European scholars, archeologists and historians with Benin art left them in awe. It was difficult for them to accept that such stylistic and advanced works in bronze and ivory could have come from a land they had regarded as uncivilized, primitive and fetish. The Benin bronze casters started creating these bronze figures before the 13th century. As a work from the ancient Benin kingdom, this piece shows signs of early contact with the Portuguese in the kingdom during the reign of Oba Esigie. In a defensive stance, the soldier positions himself well enough to enable him take a shot at a likely invader. Rendered in the typical Benin style, the eyes are stylized into oval shapes while the lips are rendered in a simplified organic shape. The soldier stands on a box and miniature soldiers are depicted standing around this box.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Untitled 2
description: Untitled 2 presents a family scene where a mother is dishing out food from a calabash to her four children. The use of form and posture captures a simple, yet intimate, moment of familial bonding, rendered in Okeke’s signature style.
artist_bio: Simon Okeke’s
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art

title: Untitled
description: Born on April 19, 1945 in Ikere-Etiti, Ekiti State, Nigeria. He earned his first degree in Fine Arts from the Ahmadu Bello University, Zaria, Nigeria in 1933. He studied Art History from Roy Sieber at Indiana University, Bloomington, Indiana from 1979-1983. He is widely known for his scholarly research and curatorial pursuits. This painting is an abstract painting showing contorted lines, figures and forms.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Gallery visitor
description: The Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art (YSMA), of the Pan-Atlantic University, Lagos, is the first purpose built, privately owned art museum in Nigeria. The 900 sqm museum hosts over 1000 artworks from Prince Yemisi Shyllon’s vast art collection. Thanks to him, the YSMA is privileged to have one of the most comprehensive and diverse art collections in the country and to make it accessible to all visitors. Our collection features artworks from established Nigerian artists including Ben Enwonwu, Uche Okeke, Aina Onabolu, El Anatsui, Erhabor Emokpae, Yusuf Grillo, Lamidi Fakeye, Bruce Onabrakpeya, Ben Osawe, Isiaka Osunde, Okpu Eze, Abayomi Barber, Gani Odutokun, Kolade Oshinowo, etc. Alongside them, are works by younger artists: Kainebi Osahenye, Ben Osaghae, Peju Layiwola, Tony Nsofor, Peju Alatise, Victor Ehikhamenor, Lanre Tejuoso, Eva Obodo, Ozioma Onuzulike, Uchay Chima, Ayobola Kekere-Ekun, Kelani Abass and many others.

title: Cycle of Life
description: Cycle of Life. The work is comprised of sections of different fabrics which have been joined together in a technique called “patchwork" which is a term borrowed from the West. It is a hand stitched wall hanging with indigo as its dominant color. The artist uses different symbolic motifs to explore different philosophies of life and to pass knowledge and meaning across. Some of the motifs signify law-making and rulers in the society. The crab motif signifies the stage in life when children crawl before they eventually walk. The flower motif represents life; the circular motifs signify money while the motif which has the form of a key signifies the tool which is used to access the money. Other motifs and their significance include: the star motif which represents the indomitable nature of a fighter; the tribal mark motif is used to show a person’s identification with his or her roots; the sugarcane motif signifies the intransigence of sweet things in life; the hand fan motif signifies royalty as well as the consuming nature of fire; and the scissors motif signifies continuity in life as it is the tool used to cut the physical connection between mother and child after birth. The motifs from the adire fabric with their symbolic meanings shows the depth of thought that goes into any adire making process.
artist_bio: Born in 1951, Nike Davies-Okundaye learnt the art of “Adire” from her mother who passed away when she was six years old and later from her grandmother who passed away a year later. Years later she worked with a popular artist in Oshogbo and here she harnessed her skills while acquiring new ones. She also attended some of the Oshogbo art workshops organized by Ulli and Georgina Beier in the early 1960s. Nike Davies-Okundaye displays the beauty of the Adire fabric in this piece which she titled the
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Oamen
description: Oamen” is a colorful piece that talks about the history and pride of the Esan tribe through the use of vibrant colours, motifs, and carefully selected materials. Aletor's art embodies the enduring power of storytelling and the universal language of creativity
artist_bio: Annehita Aletor born in 1978, is a UK-based artist of Nigerian origin. A self-taught art enthusiast with a deep connection to visual and contemporary art. She graduated from Ambrose Alli University, Ekpoma. The artwork “

title: Sculptures at the Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art
description: The Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art (YSMA), of the Pan-Atlantic University, Lagos, is the first purpose built, privately owned art museum in Nigeria. The 900 sqm museum hosts over 1000 artworks from Prince Yemisi Shyllon’s vast art collection. Thanks to him, the YSMA is privileged to have one of the most comprehensive and diverse art collections in the country and to make it accessible to all visitors. Our collection features artworks from established Nigerian artists including Ben Enwonwu, Uche Okeke, Aina Onabolu, El Anatsui, Erhabor Emokpae, Yusuf Grillo, Lamidi Fakeye, Bruce Onabrakpeya, Ben Osawe, Isiaka Osunde, Okpu Eze, Abayomi Barber, Gani Odutokun, Kolade Oshinowo, etc. Alongside them, are works by younger artists: Kainebi Osahenye, Ben Osaghae, Peju Layiwola, Tony Nsofor, Peju Alatise, Victor Ehikhamenor, Lanre Tejuoso, Eva Obodo, Ozioma Onuzulike, Uchay Chima, Ayobola Kekere-Ekun, Kelani Abass and many others.

title: The Brown Eyes Series
description: The Brown Eyes Series". This work must be part of a series of works which the artist has produced to explore the "brown eyes" as a theme.
artist_bio: Born 1972 in Lagos, Nyemike Onwuka studied general arts and majored in painting at Auchi Polytechnic, Edo State. He also studied animation and visual effects at Witwatersrand University, Johannesburg, South Africa where he did his post-graduate studies.In his paintings, the female figure features a lot as he uses it to explore and portray the disparity between the beautiful and the not so beautiful. In this piece, he portrays the beauty of a Fulani young woman. Her oval shaped eyes slant to the base of her ear while her mouth comes together to form a pout. Her head is wrapped in the scarfs typically worn by Northern women. The blotches of paint and drops gives the painting a dripping effect. The hues of brown are very evident throughout the work as the background and the lady herself have the paint dripping around them. It is highly evident that the lady's eyes are brown hence the title "
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Untitled
artist_bio: Jimoh Buraimoh is a celebrated Nigerian artist, considered a pioneer of bead painting. His work often features intricate patterns and vibrant colors, blending traditional African art forms with contemporary techniques. He has gained international recognition for his unique approach to using beads as a medium for his paintings. This untitled beadwork painting displays a series of patterns, shapely faces, and numerous colors. Buraimoh uses beads to create a textured, mosaic-like surface, highlighting his mastery of this unique art form.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art

title: To be Mindful, None Like You
description: To be Mindful, None Like You shows an elderly woman sitting in a room with a girl, who is probably her daughter. The woman has a wrapper decorated with tin black lines and patterns in blue and yellow tied to her chest and a corresponding scarf tied to her head while her daughter is dressed in a white, sleeveless blouse and a bright yellow skirt. From their outlook one could guess they are having a conversation and the woman is probably expressing her concerns, advising and admonishing her daughter to be mindful of her behavior and character as a growing girl approaching adulthood. Conversations of this nature are very important in guiding the teenage girl child to navigate the numerous challenges experienced as she grows from puberty to adulthood. It is the duty of any truly loving, caring and concerned mother to ensure her daughter avoids mistakes and finds her true purpose in life as she grows older.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Northern Setting
artist_bio: Aina Onabolu was a pioneer modernist in Nigerian art and is generally considered the father of modernism in Nigerian artdue to his service and advocacy for the formalization of art education across different levels in the nation. The northern part of Nigeria is known for its dry lands due to its proximity to the Sahara Desert. Northern setting brings to mind a lot of significant imageries; from the grazing animals to the clustered houses and from the passers-by to the rocks and the sky. The clustered houses bring to mind the unity among the northerners of Nigeria. The grazing animals tells of the good pastures of northern Nigeria despite the dry grounds in some parts. In line with Onabolu's painting style of the time, this painting shows two human figures probably returning from the farm after the day's work which shows the profound northern culture of hard work. His palette of earth brown colors successfully gives the painting a desert and northern look.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Goje Player
description: Goje Player is a classic bead painting showing a traditional musician playing the goje, a stringed instrument prominent in the Northern parts of Nigeria. In his unique style of rendition, Jimoh uses very colourful beads of different shades for this figurative expression of great aesthetic quality.
artist_bio: Born in 1943, Chief Jimoh Buraimoh attended the workshops conducted by Ulli Beier and Georgina Beier in Oshogbo in the early 1960s. He is one of the most recognized artists to have emerged from the Oshogbo Art workshops. He is best known for his remarkable bead works which appear painterly as he carefully blends in the hues of the beads in making pictorial representations.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Ola (I/4)
artist_bio: Abayomi Barber was a famous hyper-realistic sculptor and painter. After dropping out from the Yaba College of Technology in the mid 50’s, a consistent demonstration of his creativity and talent endeared him to many including his uncle, Oba Adesoji Aderemi, the then Ooni of Ife and won him a special scholarship for art studies in the UK where he lived from 1961 to 1971. Some of his notable sculptures include: the sculpture of Obafemi Awolowo, the then Governor the Western Region, done in the 60’s during his stint at the Mancini and Tozar studios in London; the sculpture of Yemoja, the mythical Yoruba goddess done for the National Gallery of Art in 1979 after his return to Nigeria; the gigantic and imposing hyper-realistic sculpture of the Ooni of Ife, Oba Aderemi Adesoji done for the National Gallery of Art in 1979, which showcased the Oba’s authority and power. In his sculptures, one sees the crafty hands and mind of Barber, his ability to explore the contours of the human form and his ingenuity in telling stories about life and people. Ola (1/4) is the first of a series of 4 post-independence sculptures of the Nigerian woman, dressed in a native attire. Her head is covered with a traditional scarf. She is probably of Yoruba decent, seemingly staring at something on her left corner. Her outlook is symbolic for its expression of elegance, grace and pride in a middle-aged, independent black woman.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Untitled 1
description: Untitled 1 presents a chaotic and dynamic composition, filled with mythical creatures and human figures. The colors—blue, yellow, and red—create a lively, energetic scene, reflecting Ekefrey’s exploration of a mythical world.
artist_bio: Emmanuel Ekefrey (b.1952) is a self-taught artist from Nigeria’s Ibibio region, known for his use of forms and figures to explore cultural myths.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art

title: Celebration
description: Celebration is a expression of an African cultural dancer performing with her arms spread in joy and happiness as she moves her feet to the rhythm of the music being played for her. Enwonwu displays his aesthetic maturity with his blend of different colours. The work also captures the concept of Negritude that is deeply rooted in his ideology as an artist.
artist_bio: Recognising the importance of heritage and culture, indigenous ritualistic dances of the Igbo people in the south-eastern region of Nigeria was a great source of influence for Ben Enwonwu.
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Ben Enwonwu Posing beside Anyanwu at the National Museum, Lagos
artist_bio: Ben Enwonwu’s teacher. It was conceptualized as a female form by Enwonwu to represent a mother who births and nurtures her child, in this case Nigeria whose independence was in sight at the time. Enwonwu said “My aim was to symbolise our rising nation. I have tried to combine material, crafts, and tradition, to express a conception that is based on womanhood—woman, the mother and nourisher of man. In our rising nation, I see the forces embodied in womanhood; the beginning, and then, the development and flowering into the fullest stature of a nation—a people!” – Ben Enwonwu. The sculpture still sits at the lawn of the museum till date.

title: Grim Polity (Ballot Boxes and Beasts of Power)
description: Grim Polity (Ballot Boxes and Beasts of Power), Tony uses rough and scratchy brush strokes of different colours – blue, white and black – to reflect on the distorted manner of the nation’s leadership and governance. Issues on godfatherism, corruption, nepotism, mafia and tribalism are addressed as political cankerworms stunting the nation’s all round growth and progress. The piece is a permanent collection of the Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art (YSMA) and is a donation by the artist.
artist_bio: Born 1973 Anthony Nsofor is a contemporary Nigerian painter. His early inspiration to become an artist came when he was a child, he admired the paintings of his uncle displayed on walls in their house in Owerri by his father. He was also fascinated by the illustrations and beautiful photographs he found in his mother’s textbooks in their home and loved copying and reproducing them exactly the way they were. He got so much applause for his creative abilities and his ingenuity from his early admirers. This spurred him to study and obtain a degree in Fine and Applied Arts from the University of Nigeria, Nsukka, majoring in painting in 1997. As a student in Nsukka, Tony was privileged to work as a studio assistant to Prof. Obiora Udechukwu, an experience he has praised for its tremendous impact in his career as an artist. Despite identifying with the Uli art philosophy of the Nsukka School of Art, he prefers to use “scribbles and scratches” for expression on his canvases rather than drawing or painting these symbols the way they are like his contemporaries. His paintings are inspired by his experiences in life, they tell great stories that are revealed and appreciated looking at them from a distance and, so far, they have been well exhibited at home and abroad. In
provenance: Yemisi Shyllon Museum of Art, Pan-Atlantic University

title: Ifa Divination Tray (Opon Ifa) 2
description: Ifa is an Orisa, deity of the Yoruba pantheon. It is regarded as the “god of destiny” in the traditional Yoruba religion and plays a practical and significant role by offering answers and solutions to existential problems in forms of ritual offerings and sacrifices to the appropriate “sub-deities”. As a higher divinity in the Yoruba pantheon, Ifa is believed to have as many as 400 deities/gods responsible for different aspects of life, living and human endeavors in general in Yorubaland. The Ifa divination system is based on a mathematical square of 16 palm nuts or cowrie shells (i.e 162 equal to 256) which are normally cast on the sand or the divination tray (known as Opon ifa or Opele) and marks subsequently made on the tray to indicate the number of shells or nuts left. All together there are 256 derivative figures or odus (gotten from the 162 shells/nuts) in the divination system, each of them functioning as interpretive keys with different esoteric meanings and the shells are repeatedly tossed to arrive at specific corresponding meanings or revelations for the questioner and his needs. In Yoruba chronology, Orunmila, the god of wisdom is believed to be the only deity that witnessed the creation of the universe by Olodumare, God almighty and, according to oral traditions, is generally considered the “keeper of God’s secrets”. Because Ifa is the first son Orunmila, he is believed to know the secrets of his father. Thus, a direct consultation with Ifa is considered a path to Orunmila himself. Surrounding the round tray are 15 well-carved birds who have gathered as spiritual observers to witness the sacred act of divination and sacrifice to Orunmila, the god of wisdom. The iconography of the inner circle comprises of: four standing, adjacent human figures bearing different gifts of sacrifice in their hands; the face of Esu or Eshu, a lower, supportive deity in the Yoruba pantheon who transforms the sacrifices of humans into food for the gods of Ifa; a worm, an insect and a walking fly (without wings) beneath the Esu on the lower part of the tray. The divination system comprises of 256 chapters, each of them containing verses known as Ese which are normally mastered and recited by the Ifa priests during divination. These verses contain myths, legends, rituals, and taboos to be appropriately prescribed as solutions for the questioner’s problems. The verses, considered the most important part of Ifa divination, are chanted or incanted by the priests in poetic language. An ifa priest can either be a man (Babalawo) or a woman (Iyalawo) and he or she must undergo rigorous training before initiation into the cult of Ifa.
